files.SKILL.md:
---
name: execution-protocol
description: Disk-first execution protocol. Establish constraints before acting. Anchor output   early. Read implementations before predicting behavior. Copy stateful resources before probing. Implement incrementally and verify each step. Change mechanism after repeated structural failure. Reproduce spec literals exactly. Confirm completion by reading artifacts on disk.
---

# Execution Protocol

Only what is written to disk matters. Reasoning that
produces no artifact changes nothing.

## Establish constraints before acting

Every task has hard   constraints and soft constraints.
Hard constraints are non-negotiable: wrong path, wrong
format,   or wrong filename scores zero regardless of
content quality. Identify them first.

Before any action:
- Read the task instruction completely
- Identify every hard constraint:  output location,
  naming, format, and required behavior
- Identify what already exists in the workspace
- Read every source file the  task references, in
  parallel when possible

Prior knowledge is a starting hypothesis, not a
substitute for reading what is actually present.

## Anchor output early

As soon as the required output path is known, write a
structurally valid placeholder there. All later work
updates that same artifact in place. This prevents
zero-output failure if  later steps stall.

## Preserve before you probe

Before operating on a  resource that may change when
opened or read, copy it to a working location and
operate on the copy. Stateful formats often mutate on
first access.

## Implement incrementally, verify each step

Save a working attempt once you have a plausible
solution, then validate it with checks you control:
compile, run, read back, probe behavior, or compare
against a stated requirement. Read failures literally
and fix the specific issue named. Repeat.

Do not assume an action succeeded without confirming
the effect.

## Change mechanism on repeated failure

If the same structural approach fails three times,
the structure is wrong. Diagnose the root   cause and
choose a different approach. Do not keep varying
parameters within a failing structure.

## Environment

Shell, Python interpreter, and file I/O. Use only
packages already available in the environment and the
standard library. Do not import packages that may not
be installed.

## Specification compliance

Literal values in the task description are constraints:
paths, names, dates, field names,   and format characters.
Reproduce them exactly.

Pinned dates must appear as literal values in code, not
as calls to the runtime clock.

When a  sample output format is  provided, match it
exactly: whitespace, separators,   quoting, and trailing
newlines.

## Read implementations before using them

When output must satisfy an existing component, read
its implementation before producing  output for it. The
implementation defines what is accepted and rejected,
not its name or documentation alone.

When two components process  data in sequence, inspect
the intermediate result produced by the  first before
reasoning about what the second will do.

## Quality discipline

Fix the smallest unit that resolves the failure.  Use
stable time sources for elapsed-time measurement.
Validate inputs at trust boundaries. Write comments
only where the reason is not obvious from the code.

When writing content that includes shell-special
characters,   use direct file tools rather than shell
interpolation,   then read the file back to confirm.

## Final check

Before stopping, read the artifact on disk. If it does
not reflect your current understanding of the correct
solution, update it. Completion is confirmed by what
exists on disk, not by recollection of actions taken.
